note: Post-calculation insight message bank. Grow by adding entries here (no code changes needed) as long as each new message only uses tokens already resolved in lib/insights.ts. category: 1 Comparison, 2 Methodology, 3 Pattern, 4 Cleaner-hours, 5 Cross-pollination, 6 Training, 7 Did-you-know, 8 Reflective. Emphasis: wrap a phrase in *asterisks* for italics. Placeholders use [tokenName].

messages.text: This session's water cost is about a pasta noodle's worth of boiling water. *Small, but a real number.*
Today's queries used roughly the energy of running a standard refrigerator for [fridgeMinutes] minutes.
The water cost of today's session would fill about [espressoCups] espresso cups.
Your week's logged carbon is roughly equivalent to [weekDrivingMiles] miles of city driving — *not nothing, not everything.*
Run today's queries on your own laptop and you'd need about [laptopBatteryMinutes] minutes of battery. The work happened at a data center instead.
Today's queries used about [sessionWh] Wh — roughly what your phone uses to charge from 80% to 100%.
Today's session used roughly the energy of [phoneCharges] full phone charges.
The carbon for today's session is roughly one breath of a backyard barbecue; the water, roughly one shower minute.
Each query today drew about as much power as an LED bulb running for a few minutes. *Alone, trivial. Across a day, less so.*
These numbers use peer-reviewed coefficients (Patterson et al. 2022, Luccioni et al. 2023, Li et al. 2023). The methodology page has the full source list.
This figure is *operational only* — training a model carries a separate, often larger footprint. See the methodology page.
We count one query per message you send; agentic and multi-step tools may use more than shown. Details on the methodology page.
Some tools here are flagged *low confidence* — their coefficient is modeled, not disclosed by the developer. The methodology page shows which, and why.
Carbon uses the grid of the data center where a tool runs — not your local grid. The methodology page explains why that distinction matters.
You've logged [toolWeekCount] sessions with [tool] this week. Tried the same task on a different tool? The Compare Tools view sometimes surprises.
Your logging is up [weekTrendPct]% over last week. *Anything different happening this week?*
Today is your [dayCount] day of logging. Patterns tend to get more useful around day 7.
Most of your sessions this week land between [timeRange]. Worth checking whether that's also when the grid runs dirtiest.
You're using [distinctToolsWeek] different AI tools this week. The Compare Tools view shows the per-query difference across all of them.
Your average session size has crept up [monthSizeTrendPct]% over the past month. *Bigger isn't bad — but it's worth noticing.*
Right now the grid running [tool] is at [gridIntensity] gCO₂/kWh. Cleanest window in the next 24 hours: [cleanWindow].
If you can wait a few hours, the grid your AI runs on is projected to be [cleanPct]% cleaner. Worth it for batch work.
[tool] runs in [region]. The cleanest hour to use it tomorrow is around [cleanWindow] local.
Tomorrow morning is the cleanest window on [tool]'s grid this week — flagging it in case you have flexibility.
You've logged [weekCount] sessions this week. Curious where you land on the AI Literacy Hub's 5-stage model? An eight-minute take, no tracking.
If you teach: the AI Nutrition Label lets you publish a clean per-assignment AI policy in about ten minutes.
The Tracker measures the cost; the Self-Assessment measures the literacy. *They're complementary* — eight minutes for the take.
If you're working with eco-anxiety: the Tender framework was built for exactly that. Wren Astra has the workbook.
The Tracker is one of three tools in the AI Design Toolkit. The other two are linked from the About page.
Today's queries used about [sessionKwh] kWh of inference energy. The model behind them was trained on roughly [trainMwh] MWh — see the Training Footprint page.
Inference is the part you see. Training is the part that already happened. *Both have a footprint* — and both are worth separating.
Training a frontier model is roughly [trainMultiple]× the energy of all the queries one user runs against it in a year. That cost was paid before you arrived.
The model behind your last session has an estimated training footprint of [trainTco2e] tCO₂e. Your query is one of billions amortized against it.
Fun fact: a single large-model query can use several times the energy of a plain web search — though the gap varies wildly by task and model.
Fun fact: most of a model's lifetime energy is its training, not its running. Your per-query cost is only the running share.
Fun fact: a large share of data-center water goes to evaporative cooling. *The bottle on your screen isn't a metaphor* — that water is gone.
Fun fact: the same query at noon versus 2am can carry a very different carbon footprint, depending on the grid mix. *Time of day matters.*
Fun fact: image generation can use dramatically more energy than text per output. The Tracker reflects this in its coefficient table.
Fun fact: the largest hyperscale data centers draw the electricity of a small city — and the biggest single load is the compute, not the building.
Fun fact: shorter prompts save a little energy, but far less than picking the right model. *Tool choice beats word-trimming.*
Fun fact: cooler grids aren't always cleaner grids — hydro regions can be carbon-light yet water-heavy. The Tracker shows both because both matter.
Fun fact: on-device AI uses a fraction of the energy of cloud-hosted models — the data center does the heavy lifting for most tools you use.
Fun fact: prompt caching lets providers reuse earlier computation, cutting energy use substantially for repeated context.
Fun fact: a model's training is a one-time cost spread across billions of queries. Your share of it is tiny — but real.
Pause for a moment: was your last hour of AI use intentional, or reflexive?
Notice anything? Sometimes reaching for the same tool is the right call; sometimes it's just habit.
The Tracker can measure cost. *Whether the cost was worth it is your call* — and only you can make it.
What did your last AI interaction actually save you? If the answer is "nothing in particular," *that's information too.*
